title: How AI Models Work
lesson:
An AI language model looks magical from the outside: you type a question, fluent text comes back. Underneath, it is doing one humble thing over and over: guessing the next token. A token is a small chunk of text — roughly a word or part of a word (we cover this in Tokens). The model reads everything so far and produces a ranked list of likely next tokens, picks one, sticks it on the end, then reads the whole thing again and guesses again. That loop is the entire engine. Everything else is detail.

The auto-complete analogy (and where it breaks)

Think of your phone's keyboard suggesting the next word. A language model is that idea taken to an extreme: trained on a huge slice of the internet, books, and code, and made enormous. The everyday word-suggester guesses from your last word or two. A real model considers thousands of earlier tokens at once. So it can keep a whole argument straight, remember a name you mentioned a page ago, and write a working function. "Fancy auto-complete" is a fair starting picture — just remember the fancy part is doing a stunning amount of work.

Three numbers it has to learn: weights

Inside the model are billions of parameters, also called weights — adjustable numbers that store everything the model "knows" (much more in Parameters). Here is a kitchen analogy. Imagine a vast stack of mixing dials. Each dial nudges how much one piece of information affects the next. Training is the slow process of turning all those dials until the recipe — text in, sensible text out — comes out right. A modern model has anywhere from a few billion to hundreds of billions of these dials.

How the dials get set: training

Training works by guess-and-correct, millions of times. The model is shown real text with the next word hidden, makes a guess, and is told how wrong it was. That wrongness is a single number called the loss (how far the guess was from the truth). A method called gradient descent (figuring out which direction to nudge each dial to shrink the loss) then tweaks every weight a tiny bit. Repeat across trillions of tokens and the dials settle into a setting that predicts text remarkably well. This whole phase is Training vs Inference — training builds the model; inference is later, when you actually use it.

From scores to words: logits and temperature

For each step, the model outputs a raw score for every possible next token. Those raw scores are called logits (the unpolished numbers before they become probabilities). A step called softmax squashes them into percentages that add up to 100%. So the model might think: the 41%, a 22%, my 9%, and so on. Then it samples one. A setting called temperature controls how adventurous that pick is — low temperature almost always grabs the top choice (safe, repetitive); high temperature gives long-shots a real chance (creative, riskier).

import torch

logits = torch.tensor([3.1, 1.4, 0.9])      # raw scores for 3 tokens
temperature = 0.7                            # lower = more focused
probs = torch.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=0)
print(probs)   # e.g. [0.78, 0.14, 0.08] -> sums to 1.0
next_token = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)     # sample one

That tiny snippet is the heart of generation. Divide the logits by temperature, turn them into probabilities, draw one. Do it again for the next token, and the next.

What does the guessing? The architecture

The machine producing those logits is almost always a Transformer. Its key trick is the Attention Mechanism — at each step the model decides which earlier tokens matter most right now, the way a reader's eyes jump back to a key clue. To do this with numbers, words are first turned into lists of numbers called Embeddings, so "king" and "queen" land near each other in meaning-space. You do not need the wiring yet; just hold the shape of it.

⚠️ The model has no live database and no built-in fact-checker. It only predicts plausible text. When a confident-sounding answer is simply wrong, that is a hallucination — a side effect of "plausible" not meaning "true."

Why this matters in real products

Once you see the loop, real behavior stops being mysterious. Streaming output appears word-by-word because tokens really are produced one at a time. The same prompt can give different answers because sampling involves chance. Replies cost more when longer because each token is another full pass. And to make a model better at your task, you either change its inputs (Prompt Engineering, RAG) or gently re-set its dials on your own examples (Fine-Tuning Basics). Same engine — you are just steering it.
mentalModel: A language model is a giant next-token guessing machine: read everything so far, score every possible next chunk, pick one, repeat. Billions of pre-tuned dials make those guesses startlingly good — but they are still guesses, not lookups.
mistakes: Believing the model "looks things up." It does not consult a database; it predicts plausible text from patterns it absorbed in training. That is exactly why it can state wrong facts with total confidence (a hallucination).

Expecting the same prompt to give the same answer. Because generation samples from probabilities, replies vary run to run. Set temperature to 0 (or near it) when you need repeatable, deterministic output.
Thinking bigger always means smarter. More parameters can help, but a smaller, well-tuned model often wins on a narrow task — and runs faster and cheaper.

Confusing training with inference. Training is the one-time, expensive dial-setting phase. Inference is every cheap, fast use afterward. Chatting with a model does not teach it; it forgets everything once the conversation ends.

Treating temperature as a "smartness" knob. It only controls randomness, not intelligence. High temperature makes output more varied (and more likely to wander); low temperature makes it focused and repetitive.

Assuming the model reasons before answering. A plain model commits to each token as it goes. Getting it to "think first" (writing out steps, or using reasoning models) is a technique you apply, not a default it has.
exercise:
Open any chat model you can access. Send the exact same prompt three times in a fresh conversation each time — for example: Write a one-line slogan for a coffee shop. Note whether the three answers differ. Then, if your tool exposes a temperature setting, run it once at 0 and once at 1.0 and compare how varied and how 'safe' the outputs feel. Write two sentences explaining what you observed in terms of next-token sampling.

Stretch goal: In Python, build the 3-token softmax example from the lesson. Loop over temperatures 0.2, 0.7, and 1.5, print the probability list each time, and describe in one line how the gap between the top choice and the others changes as temperature rises.
solution: You should see the three plain runs give different slogans — proof that generation samples from a probability list rather than returning one fixed answer. At temperature 0 the outputs become nearly identical and a bit bland (the model almost always takes its top pick); at 1.0 they get more varied and surprising, sometimes odd. For the stretch goal, as temperature rises the probabilities flatten out: at 0.2 the top token might be ~0.95, at 1.5 it could drop toward ~0.55 while the long-shots climb. Lower temperature = sharper, more confident distribution; higher = flatter, more exploratory. Same logits, different shaping.
summary: An AI model generates text by repeatedly guessing the next token: it scores every possible token (logits), turns those into probabilities (softmax), and samples one — then loops. Billions of trained parameters (weights), set during training by shrinking a loss, power those guesses; using the model afterward is inference. Temperature shapes how random the picks are. It predicts plausible text, not verified facts — which is why it streams, varies, and sometimes hallucinates.

keyTerms.def: The small chunk of text — roughly a word or word-piece — that a model reads and writes one at a time.
One of the billions of adjustable numbers inside a model that store what it has learned. Training is the act of setting them.
The slow, costly phase where the model is shown text, guesses, is corrected, and has its weights nudged to predict better.
Actually using a finished model to generate text. It is fast and cheap compared to training, and does not change the weights.
A single number measuring how wrong a guess was. Training works by steadily shrinking this number across huge amounts of text.
The raw, unpolished scores a model assigns to every possible next token before they are turned into probabilities.
The step that converts logits into probabilities that add up to 100%, so one token can be sampled fairly.
A knob controlling randomness in sampling. Low values make output focused and repeatable; high values make it varied and creative.
When a model produces fluent, confident text that is simply false — a side effect of predicting plausible words rather than checking facts.
